1: Psalm 128 is a covenant beatitude psalm — ashre (how blessed/happy) opening the description of the ideal covenant household. "How blessed is every person (kol-ish) who fears the LORD (yere YHWH) and walks in his ways" — the blessing is inclusive: "every person" is the scope, not merely the particularly devout or the king. "Fear of the LORD" is the foundational wisdom disposition: not terror but reverential awe, the orientation of the whole person toward YHWH as the source of all wisdom and the ground of all right living (Proverbs 1:7; Psalms 9:10; Psalms 111:10). "Walks in his ways (holech bidrakhav)" — the walking metaphor for covenant obedience: not a static position but a continuous, directional movement through life in conformity with the covenant pattern.
2: "You will enjoy the fruit of your labor (yegiah kapecha); you will be blessed, and things will go well for you" — the covenant blessing of productive labor: the fruit of one's work will be enjoyed by the worker. This is the reversal of several biblical curses: the Eden curse (Genesis 3:17-19: others will eat what you labor for) and the covenant curse of Deuteronomy 28:30-33 (you will plant vineyards but not drink the wine; others will eat your crops). The blessed household is one where the connection between labor and its fruit is intact: the worker receives what the work produces. Yegiah kapecha ("the labor of your hands/palms") — the word yegiah can carry the weight of toil, effort, and even hardship; the blessing is not that the labor is effortless but that its fruit is received.
3: "Your wife will be like a fruitful vine (gefen poriyyah) flourishing within your home; your children like young olive shoots (shitele zeitim) gathered around your table" — two agricultural images for the blessed household. The vine (gefen) as image of the productive wife: vines require careful tending, are vulnerable in early years, and become remarkably productive over time; they produce not just food but the wine of celebration. The olive shoots (shitele zeitim) as image of children: young olive trees gathered in a cluster around the central trunk, the household table as the gathering point. Both images are agricultural realities that the Judean household would recognize: the vineyard and the olive grove were the primary sources of covenant-land prosperity.
4: "This is the blessing that comes to the man who fears the LORD (yere YHWH)" — the verse functions as a summary conclusion for Psalms 128:2-3 before expanding the horizon in Psalms 128:5-6. The repetition of "fears the LORD" brackets the household-blessing section (Psalms 128:1 and 4) and confirms that the material blessing of the household is the covenant blessing — not a general prosperity gospel but the specific covenant promise attached to the specific covenant orientation of fear-of-YHWH. The Deuteronomic covenant structure (Deuteronomy 28:1-14) specifies exactly this: obedience to YHWH's ways produces material blessing in children, crops, and flocks. Psalm 128 is the liturgical celebration of this covenant dynamic.
5: "May the LORD bless you from Zion (yevarechecha YHWH miTziyyon); may you see Jerusalem flourish all the days of your life" — the blessing extends from the household to the city. The source of blessing is Zion — the temple-mountain where YHWH's presence dwells — and the blessing flows outward from there to the individual household. The pilgrim who has come from the individual household to Zion is given the blessing of Zion to take back to the household: the movement is bidirectional. "See Jerusalem flourish (urine betov Yerushalayim)" — the pilgrimage city's wellbeing is the frame within which the individual household flourishes; personal blessing and covenant community blessing are not separable in the Psalter's theology.
6: "May you live to see your grandchildren (benei vanecha). Peace be upon Israel (shalom al-Yisra'el)" — the temporal extension of the blessing: long enough to see the third generation, grandchildren, is the sign of maximal covenant blessing in the OT (Genesis 50:23: Joseph saw Ephraim's children to the third generation; Job 42:16: Job saw four generations). The blessing extends from individual (v1-2) to household (v3-4) to city (v5) to nation (v6). "Peace be upon Israel" closes with the same covenant-community blessing that closes Psalms 125:5 — the individual pilgrimage blessing is always ultimately corporate: the personal flourishing of the covenant household contributes to and participates in the shalom of the whole covenant people.
